skill.name: sql-queries
skill.description: Expert SQL query generation for DBX Studio. Use when writing, optimizing, or debugging SQL queries against user database connections.
skill.summary: Write, optimize, and debug dialect-aware SQL queries for DBX Studio database connections.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, SQL keywords, and documented DBX Studio tool names as dangerous patterns. Manual review found no executable scripts, shell commands, weak cryptographic code, reconnaissance logic, network access, or prompt injection attempts.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQL Examples Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are fenced SQL examples and inline DBX Studio tool names, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the cited locations contain SQL code fences or literal tool names. No shell interpreter, Ruby execution context, or command invocation is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQL Description Terms Flagged as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts correspond to SQL description text, DESC ordering, or describe_table documentation, not hashing or encryption code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is SQL guidance and table-inspection terminology. There is no cryptographic API, algorithm selection, or secret-handling code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: SQL Window Function Flagged as Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system reconnaissance alert points to a SQL window function example, not host inspection or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location contains an aggregate window expression for running totals. No operating system, network, process, or file-system reconnaissance behavior is present.

content.user_title: Build Safer DBX Studio SQL Queries
content.value_statement: Database work often fails when queries ignore schema details or SQL dialect differences. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to inspect schema first, write bounded SELECT queries, and explain results clearly.
actual_capabilities: Guides SQL generation across PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, MySQL, and SQLite.
Recommends schema inspection before writing queries against user database connections.
Encourages bounded SELECT and WITH queries with automatic LIMIT usage.
Provides examples for CTEs, aggregations, and window functions.
Defines response formatting for direct answers and optional SQL display.
limitations: Does not include executable scripts, connectors, or database client code.
Depends on DBX Studio tools for schema reads, table previews, and query execution.
Restricts execution guidance to SELECT and WITH statements.
Does not define migration, write, update, delete, or permission-management workflows.

use_cases.title: Analyze Business Metrics
use_cases.description: Create bounded aggregation queries for revenue, orders, users, and activity trends.

use_cases.title: Debug Query Results
use_cases.description: Inspect schema details and adjust joins, filters, or window functions for accurate output.

use_cases.title: Explore Connected Databases
use_cases.description: Preview tables and generate dialect-aware SELECT queries before deeper analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Preview a Table
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the schema for my connected database, preview the relevant table, and show the first useful question I can answer with it.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Safe Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a dialect-aware SELECT query for monthly revenue. Validate the table and column names first, and include a reasonable row limit.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize an Existing Query
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this SQL query for correctness and performance. Explain the changes, keep it read-only, and preserve the intended result.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Complex Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use schema inspection to build a CTE-based analysis with ranking, aggregation, and clear result interpretation for the selected database.

output_examples.input: How many orders did we receive this month?
output_examples.output: The assistant inspects the orders table, runs a bounded read-only query, and returns the monthly order count with the relevant date filter.
The assistant validates customer and order columns, uses a ranking approach, and explains which records represent the latest orders.

output_examples.input: Why is my revenue query returning duplicate totals?
output_examples.output: The assistant checks join keys and table grain, then recommends a corrected aggregation strategy.

best_practices: Inspect schema and column details before generating SQL.
Use SELECT or WITH queries and add LIMIT unless full output is required.
Quote identifiers and match the SQL dialect for the active connection.
anti_patterns: Do not run write, update, delete, migration, or permission-changing statements.
Do not guess table names or column names without checking schema tools.
Do not return large unbounded result sets unless the user explicitly requests them.

faq.question: Which databases does this skill support?
faq.answer: It provides guidance for PostgreSQL, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, MySQL, and SQLite.

faq.question: Can this skill modify database records?
faq.answer: No. The documented execution guidance is limited to SELECT and WITH queries.

faq.question: Does it execute SQL by itself?
faq.answer: No. It instructs the assistant to use DBX Studio tools when those tools are available.

faq.question: How does it reduce query mistakes?
faq.answer: It tells the assistant to read schema details and validate table or column names before writing queries.

faq.question: Can it explain query results?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks the assistant to answer directly and present numbers clearly after running read-only queries.

faq.question: Is it useful for optimization?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide query restructuring with CTEs, aggregations, and window functions.
